---
title: "Glossary"
subtitle: "Every term used on this site, in one plain-language line"
---

New here? The [Foundations](foundations.qmd) page explains these in context and in order. This page
is for quick lookups. Terms are grouped, then alphabetical within a group.

## Core concepts

**Annotation** — a label drawn onto data (a box, mask, or class) marking the correct answer; also
the act of drawing it.

**Backbone** — the main body of a network that turns an image into a rich numerical summary
(features); a small "head" on top produces the task-specific answer.

**Channel** — one colour layer of an image. Colour images have three (Red, Green, Blue).

**Checkpoint** — a saved file of a model's trained weights (e.g. `yolo26n.pt`). Load it to skip
training and run the model.

**CNN (Convolutional Neural Network)** — the classic image architecture; slides small pattern
filters across the image, building edges → textures → parts → objects.

**Deep learning** — machine learning with neural networks that have many layers.

**Embedding (vector)** — a list of numbers summarising an image/patch/word such that similar things
get similar lists. Comparing things becomes comparing vectors.

**Epoch** — one full pass of training over the entire training dataset.

**Feature** — a piece of information a model has extracted from the input (e.g. "there's an edge
here," or, deeper, "this looks like fur").

**Fine-tuning** — continuing to train a pretrained model on your own smaller dataset to specialise
it. Much cheaper than training from scratch.

**Ground truth** — the known-correct answer for an example, used to train or score a model.

**GPU / VRAM** — the graphics processor that runs these models fast by doing massive parallel math;
VRAM is its memory, which limits how big a model fits (here, 24 GB).

**Head** — the small final part of a network that converts backbone features into the task output
(a class, boxes, a depth map…).

**Inference** — running a trained model to get answers on new inputs. (The "use" phase, vs.
training.)

**Label** — the correct answer attached to a training example (e.g. "cat").

**Latency** — time to process one input, usually in milliseconds (ms). Lower is faster.

**Layer** — one stage of a neural network; data flows through many in sequence.

**Loss** — a number measuring how wrong the model's prediction is; training works to make it small.

**Model** — a mathematical function with many adjustable numbers (weights) that maps an input to an
output.

**Neural network** — a model built from many simple units in layers, loosely inspired by neurons.

**Parameter / weight** — one of the millions of adjustable numbers inside a model; training sets
their values.

**Pretrained** — a model whose weights were already trained (often by a big lab on huge data),
ready to download and use.

**Tensor** — a multi-dimensional grid of numbers; an image is a height × width × 3 tensor.

**Training** — the learning phase: adjusting weights from labelled examples so the model gets less
wrong over time.

**Transformer** — an architecture built on "attention," where every part of the input can directly
relate to every other part; now dominant in vision and language.

**Attention** — the transformer's core operation: letting each element decide which other elements
are relevant to it.

**ViT (Vision Transformer)** — a transformer applied to images by splitting the picture into patches
and letting them attend to each other.

## Tasks

**Classification** — assign one label to a whole image.

**Object detection** — find objects and draw a labelled box around each.

**Segmentation** — label every pixel; **instance** segmentation separates individual objects,
**semantic** segmentation labels by category.

**Depth estimation** — predict how far each pixel is from the camera. **Monocular** = from a single
image.

**Pose estimation / keypoints** — locate body joints and connect them into a skeleton.

**Tracking (MOT, Multi-Object Tracking)** — follow the same objects across video frames, giving each
a persistent ID.

**Retrieval** — find the images most similar to a query, using embeddings.

**VLM (Vision-Language Model)** — a model that takes an image (and text) and produces text:
captions, answers, reasoning.

## Detection & evaluation terms

**Anchor** — a pre-defined template box older detectors adjust to fit objects; "anchor-free" models
skip them.

**Bounding box (box)** — a rectangle around an object, given as four numbers (left, top, right,
bottom).

**Confidence** — the model's own 0–1 certainty in a prediction; you threshold it to decide what to
keep.

**COCO** — a large standard dataset of everyday photos (80 object classes) used to train and compare
detectors. "COCO mAP" is the standard detection score.

**False positive / false negative** — a wrong detection (saw something that wasn't there) / a miss
(failed to see something that was).

**FPS (frames per second) / throughput** — how many images a model processes per second; ~30 FPS is
real-time.

**IoU (Intersection over Union)** — overlap between a predicted and true box: shared area ÷ combined
area. 1 = perfect.

**Keypoint** — a single labelled point, e.g. "right knee," in pose estimation.

**mAP (mean Average Precision)** — the standard detection score (0–1): area under the
precision–recall curve, averaged over classes (and, for COCO, over IoU thresholds 0.5–0.95).

**Mask** — a per-pixel yes/no marking exactly which pixels belong to an object.

**NMS (Non-Maximum Suppression)** — clean-up step that removes duplicate overlapping boxes for the
same object, keeping the most confident. "NMS-free" models avoid needing it.

**OKS (Object Keypoint Similarity)** — the IoU-equivalent for pose: how well predicted joints match
the true ones; the basis of pose mAP.

**Precision** — of the model's predictions, the fraction that were correct (penalises false alarms).

**Recall** — of the real objects, the fraction the model found (penalises misses).

**mIoU (mean IoU)** — the standard segmentation score: average pixel-overlap across classes.

**HOTA / MOTA / IDF1** — tracking scores measuring how well IDs stay correct across frames;
**ID-switch** = a tracked object wrongly getting a new ID.

## Modern / foundation-model terms

**CLIP** — a model trained to put matching images and text captions near each other in one embedding
space, enabling zero-shot classification and retrieval.

**Foundation model** — a large model pretrained broadly and reused for many tasks, often by
prompting, instead of trained for one task.

**Open-vocabulary** — detect/segment by free-text prompt ("find the ladder") rather than a fixed
class list.

**Prompt** — the instruction you give a promptable model: text (CLIP, VLMs, Grounding DINO), a point
or box (SAM), etc.

**Pseudo-label** — a label produced *by another model* instead of a human; cheap but noisier. Used
to auto-build training data in [module 10](modules/10-finetuning/report.qmd).

**Zero-shot** — applying a model to categories/tasks it was never explicitly trained on, via
description.

## Tooling you'll see named

**ultralytics** — the popular library that packages YOLO models for easy training and inference
(AGPL-3.0 licensed).

**transformers (Hugging Face)** — the standard library for downloading and running a huge range of
models with one API.

**supervision** — a utility library used here to hold detections/keypoints and draw them on images.

**FiftyOne** — a tool for downloading, browsing, and evaluating datasets (used to fetch COCO
subsets).

**Quarto** — the tool that turns these reports into the website you're reading.

**uv** — the Python package/environment manager used to install everything reproducibly.
